464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented flow from sign-up → first class → integrated into community (this is your conversion funnel leaking retention)
- **Churn & cancellation**: no protocol for identifying at-risk members, win-back triggers, or exit interviews (critical revenue defence)
- **Coaching standards & quality**: no audit checklist, class observation protocol, or feedback loop (directly impacts NPS and retention)
- **Coaching development**: no roadmap for progression, certification tracking, or performance management (staff turnover risk)
- **Member check-ins**: no cadence or framework for proactive retention touchpoints (low-touch, high-impact)
- **GDPR & data privacy**: no documented safeguards for member data handling, consent management, or incident response (compliance + trust)
- **Pause & suspension**: no member lifecycle management for temporary holds vs. cancellation (revenue visibility)
- **Referral & milestone programmes**: no structured incentive mechanics or tracking (growth lever not being systematised)
- **Supply chain (order & manage supplies/sundries)**: no vendor relationships, stock levels, or reorder triggers (operational friction point)
- **Troubleshooting guide**: no runbook for common member complaints, billing issues, or facility problems (avoids inconsistent resolution)
- **Health & safety incidents**: no incident log, escalation, or follow-up protocol (legal & duty-of-care exposure)

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coaches SOPs (20/24)**: likely missing performance metrics, session planning templates, and real-time feedback capture; no link to member outcomes tracking
- **Weekly Key Coach Tasks**: exists conceptually but no written spec—needs task checklist, timing, accountability owner, and quality gates
- **Class-to-outcome mapping**: no SOP connecting class themes to member transformation

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

**1. Arrest the collapse in member value & retention**
June shows 147 active clients but AR/M crashed to £120.48 (down 27% from May's £165.56). Churn hit 0% in June but *after* losing 25 members month-on-month (May: 122 → June: 147 looks like intake, but baseline dropped). Your new studio capacity should support 180+ members profitably; you're underutilising it. Immediately audit why per-member revenue fell so hard: are pricing tiers misaligned, are newer intake members on discount rates, or are ancillary upsells (PT, nutrition, retail, class packs) not being pushed? This is worth £5–8k/month at scale.

**2. Stabilise member churn — it's volatile and bleeding profit**
April–June averaged 4.7% monthly churn vs September–November's 2.5%. January hit 7.45%, March 6.6%, April 8.33%. A 50-person base losing 5% per month = 2.5 members; at £165 AR/M average, that's £412/month lost revenue *before* replacement cost. Implement a retention playbook: exit interviews for every leaver, re-engagement email sequences for at-risk members (low class attendance), and a win-back offer. Target: <2% churn, month-on-month.

**3. Rebuild class utilisation with dynamic pricing & bundling**
You grew to 147 members (a 73% increase from September's 85) but revenue flatlined at £17.7k vs September's £13.8k — that's only 28% growth. More members should mean higher revenue. Classes are likely not full, or members are on unlimited plans underutilising peak slots. Split pricing by peak/off-peak (6am, 12pm, 5–7pm vs 10am, 3pm); offer 4-pack class bundles at £45 (vs drop-in at £12), and create member ambassador tier (free/discounted classes for referrals). This unlocks £2–4k/month.

---

# EXPENSES — CRITICAL ISSUE IN JUNE

**4. June non-people expenses spiked to £9,390 — investigate the £655 variance**
May: £9,233 non-people costs. June: £9,390 (+£157, +1.7%). But more important: April–May average £9,872 (post-move, stabilising); June should be tracking at £9–

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes to Eilis and JoJo:** Grace runs 17 Build: Lower, 9 Build: Upper, 6 Elevate, and 3 Strongevity. Assign Build: Lower to Eilis (strength in her wheelhouse), Upper and Elevate to JoJo, and Strongevity to either coach on rotation. This removes ~35 coaching sessions from Grace immediately.

• **Move Grace's 3 No Sweat Intros to a rota.** These can be shared across both coaches or handled by whichever one is coaching that day, reducing Grace's load further without creating bottlenecks.

• **Ease JoJo's PT load by bringing Eilis up to 15–18 PTs per month.** JoJo carries 44 PTs; Eilis has only 11. Pair Eilis with experienced clients and gradually build her confidence and case load. This protects JoJo from burnout and makes better use of available capacity.

• **Assign Body Composition Scans to a standing rota.** These are admin tasks, not coaching. Rotate them evenly (6–7 per coach per month) rather than letting them cluster with PT bookings. This frees up ~8–10 hours per coach per month for actual coaching.

• **Create a Tuesdays backup.** JoJo always coaches Tuesdays, but having Eilis shadow or co-coach one session per month will build her confidence on that technically demanding day, reducing single-point-of-failure risk.

• **Monitor Eilis's class load after redistribution.** Once she takes on Build: Lower and more PTs, she'll be closer to 70–75 sessions; if she stays under 100, she has capacity to absorb more Circuits or Capacity classes

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings** — position it between your quietest PT hours and evening classes. Charge £5-8 per session or bundle 5 passes. This captures members who want structure-free lifting time with a coach nearby for form checks, filling dead air and generating incremental revenue.

2. **Run a monthly "Barbell Clinic" workshop on Saturday mornings** (your quietest day) — 45 mins focused on one lift (deadlift, squat, bench). Price at £15-20 and invite Barbelle attendees plus members who struggle with heavy compound lifts. Builds confidence for the actual Barbelle class.

**For quieter classes:**

3. **Shift Engine to Wednesday or Friday at lunch or 6pm** — it's performing badly (47 and 15 attendees in 90/30 days) likely due to poor timing, not the class itself. Test it on your busiest days when momentum and foot traffic are highest. Pair with a 2-week "invite everyone who's done a conditioning class" push to frame it as the next step.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Start with revenue reality, not perfection** — List every service The Forge currently delivers (group classes, PT, challenges, merchandise, etc.). Assign actual revenue per service from last 3 months. This forces you to see what's actually selling vs. what you *think* should sell. Menu clarity follows revenue truth, not aspirations.

• **Build two separate docs, one brutal edit** — Coaches need: service name + exact deliverables + what you say NO to (e.g., "PT is 1:1 only, not group coaching"). Members need: benefit-led headlines + pricing + what's genuinely included vs. upsells. Don't use the same language for both — coaches need operational clarity, members need desire.

• **Price anchor to your best performer** — Identify your highest-margin or highest-volume service (probably group membership or PT packages). Price everything else relative to that. If group classes are £40/month, PT sessions shouldn't be ambiguous—make them £X per session or £Y/month bundles. Kill "contact for pricing" entirely.

• **Test the menu internally first (48 hours max)** — Give coaches the service doc, ask them: "Can you sell this? Is anything missing or confusing?" Iterate once based on feedback. This catches gaps before members see it and prevents "our coaches told us different rates" situations.

• **Publish as a simple, one-page visual** — Not a 20-page PDF. Use it on your website, WhatsApp, and printed in-gym. If it takes explaining, it's too complicated.

What's your highest-revenue service right now?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
## Insights

**You've cracked lead volume at rock-bottom cost, but conversion has collapsed.** July pulled 90 leads at £3/lead—your best cost efficiency ever—but only 3 converted (3% close rate). Compare April: 69 leads at £6/lead with 29% close rate. You're now acquiring cheap leads that aren't sales-ready; the ad is resonating with the wrong audience or the wrong stage of buyer.

**May and June show the inverse problem happened first.** Cost-per-lead climbed to £14–£21 while close rates also dropped (61% → 65%). This suggests audience fatigue and creative decay. July's spend cut (£241, down 42% from June) combined with a new creative or broader targeting to fix volume—and it worked for volume, but the leads are colder.

**Your profit collapsed despite lower spend.** July made £458 profit vs April's £1,128. The lead-gen engine is now optimising for the wrong metric: you're chasing cheap leads instead of cheap *sales*.

---

## Actions

**1. Audit your July creative and targeting immediately.** You've almost certainly widened the audience (age, interests, location radius) or changed the ad creative to hit volume. Pull the July creative assets and audience specs side-by-side with April's top performer. If you've broadened targeting, narrow it back. If creative changed, revert or test April's top performer again. The 90 leads tell you reach grew; close rate tells you relevance tanked.

**2. Layer a lead quality filter into your funnel before sales handoff.** Don't optimise Meta to "leads"—optimise to qualified leads. Add a qualification step: email confirmation, or a simple one-question form ("What's your main fitness goal?") on the landing page. This costs almost nothing and will either show you your ad is attracting genuinely unqualified traffic (in which case, fix targeting), or reveal a sales/follow-up process failure. Budget £20–30 to test this; it'll clarify everything.

**3. Restart the July budget at April's targeting and creative, but split-test messaging angles.** Run £120/week across: (a) "

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REVIEW

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY: ARREST THE CHURN SPIKE & LIFT REV/MEMBER

- **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May (vs 3.5% in April); June recovered to 3.3% but damage done.** You lost ~17 members in May alone. Root cause: post-relocation transition friction, class scheduling disruption, or billing/comms glitch? Audit cancellation reasons in TeamUp immediately. June's stabilisation is good, but you're at 143 members vs 138 potential without May bleed. **Action: win back 10 churned members via personal outreach (free month, relaunch email); fix whatever caused May's exodus within 7 days.**

- **Rev/member has flatlined at £110–138, trending down to £110 in June.** This should be climbing post-relocation (bigger space = premium pricing room). You're not monetising growth. **Action: introduce a tiered pricing model — keep base at current rate, launch a "Premium/Peak hours" tier at +20% (£25–30/month uplift target); audit current pricing vs Belfast market and comparable UK gyms; identify 20–30 members willing to upgrade to classes you've added in the new studio.**

- **Membership-only revenue obscures true pricing power.** Are you running class passes, PT packages, or merchandise? Last 3 months avg £15,547 rev / 140 members ≈ £111/head is thin. **Action: launch a one-off "New studio reopening offer" — 6-month lock-in at +15% with 2 PT sessions bundled; target 15–20 sign-ups at £180/month (vs £130 base). This alone adds £2,700–3,600 monthly run-rate.**

---

## EXPENSES — CASH DRAIN: JUNE EXPENSES AT £20.6K ON £15.8K REVENUE

- **Last 3 months (May–Jun) cash out averaged £18,642 vs revenue £15,880. You're running a £2,762/month structural deficit (excluding April relocation).** This is unsustainable. June was the worst: £20.6k out on £15.8k in = **margin of -31% before rent/utilities/payroll are even visible.** **Action: break down the May–June £16.6–20.6k cash-outs by category (payroll, rent, supplies, utilities). If payroll >60% of revenue, you're overstaff
